questions.prompt: An ideal low-pass filter with bandwidth B has |H(ω)| = 1 for:
questions.explanation: The ideal LPF passes all frequencies with magnitude up to B. Since the response is symmetric about ω=0 for real systems, the passband extends from −B to +B, i.e. |ω| ≤ B.

questions.prompt: Which filter type blocks a specific range of frequencies and passes everything else?
questions.explanation: The band-stop (or notch) filter blocks frequencies in a band B₁ ≤ |ω| ≤ B₂ and passes all other frequencies. It's the complement of the band-pass filter.
The high-pass filter blocks frequencies below the cutoff B. It has zero magnitude for |ω| < B and unity magnitude for |ω| ≥ B.

questions.prompt: An ideal BPF has cutoffs B₁ = 100 rad/s and B₂ = 500 rad/s. What is the bandwidth of the passband?
questions.explanation: The bandwidth of a band-pass filter is B₂ − B₁ = 500 − 100 = 400 rad/s. This is the width of the frequency band that the filter passes.

questions.prompt: If you cascade an ideal LPF (cutoff 1000 rad/s) with an ideal HPF (cutoff 200 rad/s), the result behaves as:
questions.explanation: The HPF blocks everything below 200 rad/s, the LPF blocks everything above 1000 rad/s. What survives is the band from 200 to 1000 rad/s — a band-pass response.

questions.prompt: Why is the ideal filter magnitude response symmetric about ω = 0?
options: Because the system's impulse response is real-valued
Because the filter has linear phase
questions.correctAnswer: Because the system's impulse response is real-valued
questions.explanation: For any system with a real-valued impulse response h(t), the Fourier transform satisfies H(−ω) = H*(ω), which means |H(−ω)| = |H(ω)|. This symmetry is a fundamental property of real systems.

questions.prompt: An ideal LPF with B = 5 kHz feeding into music that has frequencies from 20 Hz to 20 kHz will output:
options: All frequencies from 20 Hz to 20 kHz
Only frequencies from 20 Hz to 5 kHz
questions.correctAnswer: Only frequencies from 20 Hz to 5 kHz
questions.explanation: The LPF passes everything at or below 5 kHz with unity gain and completely blocks everything above 5 kHz. The output retains the 20 Hz to 5 kHz range only.

questions.prompt: Which pair of ideal filters, when cascaded, produces a band-stop response?
questions.explanation: When the HPF cutoff is higher than the LPF cutoff, the HPF blocks the low frequencies, the LPF blocks the high frequencies, and neither passes the band between them — but this actually gives zero output. A true BSF requires a parallel (sum) combination, not cascade. This illustrates the subtlety of filter design.

questions.prompt: For the sinusoidal input x(t) = 3cos(200t + π/4) through an ideal LPF with B = 150 rad/s, the output is:
questions.explanation: The input frequency is ω₀ = 200 rad/s > B = 150 rad/s. Since |H(200)| = 0 for the ideal LPF, the output y(t) = 3 × 0 × cos(...) = 0. The sinusoid is completely blocked.

questions.prompt: The parameter B in the ideal LPF definition represents:
questions.explanation: B is the bandwidth of the ideal LPF — it defines the boundary between the passband (|ω| ≤ B) and the stopband (|ω| > B). All frequencies below B pass at unity gain.

questions.prompt: How many cutoff frequency parameters are needed to specify an ideal band-pass filter?
questions.explanation: A band-pass filter needs two cutoff frequencies: B₁ (lower edge) and B₂ (upper edge). The passband is B₁ ≤ |ω| ≤ B₂. LPF and HPF need only one cutoff each.

questions.prompt: The magnitude response of an ideal filter is always either 0 or 1 because:
options: It is a physical requirement of all filters
This is the mathematical definition of 'ideal' — no partial attenuation
Practical filters can only achieve 0 or 1
The transfer function has unity numerator
questions.correctAnswer: This is the mathematical definition of 'ideal' — no partial attenuation
questions.explanation: The word 'ideal' means the filter either passes a frequency perfectly (gain = 1) or blocks it completely (gain = 0). Real filters always have some partial attenuation in the transition region, which is why they are non-ideal.
